name: Cova dets Ases
starting_point: Upon entering Porto Colom from the Felanitx road, find the roundabout with a ship's anchor. Follow Calle s'Algar towards the lighthouse until the road turns sharply right and the sea appears, with a small green area for parking.
best_season: Any season, though summer is recommended for swimming at s'Algar
access: Bus service to Porto Colom depending on origin
equipment: Neoprene required for swimming in the lake
description: A 497m-long cave connecting to the sea through two siphons. Features a large entrance chamber, a decorated room with columns and flowstones containing animal bones, a crystal-clear lake, and narrow passages leading to a labyrinth area. New galleries were discovered in 2014 and are now protected by a locked gate.
full_description:
Mallorca, despite its small size, possesses important and varied caves of high interest for underwater explorations. The Cova des Ases connects to the sea after passing through two siphons; the first, eleven meters long, connects to a 117 m chamber with a lake at its SE end. The lake, after 20 m of flooded gallery, connects to the open sea. The total length of the cavity is 497 m. In a straight line toward the sea from the entrance, the length is approximately 326 m (measured with SigPac).

The entrance to the cave is hidden among bushes. The visit begins once through the entrance, descending a considerable slope over ground loaded with rocks of different sizes, which however poses no danger as it is completely dry.

The dimensions of this first chamber are considerable but devoid of formations. If we look carefully, just before reaching the lowest part, a door opens to the left, and once we peer through it, a sort of corridor can be seen a few meters below that appears to continue, but which for now has remained pending exploration.

The large entrance chamber connects to a very small one where the cave seems to end, but if we look to the left side, some formations can be seen a few meters up, and possibly some paint marks in the shape of arrows indicating the continuation of this cave. We therefore have to climb up a ramp that is a bit slippery at first but is not at all dangerous. We continue climbing with a tendency to the left, and shortly after, we notice a strong light entering from outside, which might make us think we are exiting the cave, but this is not the case — it is a circular opening of more than one meter in diameter in the cave ceiling where a small fig tree can be seen. The coordinates of this opening are 523570 E, 4365007 N UTM DATUM EUROPEAN 1950, located 55 meters from the main entrance in the direction of the sea, slightly displaced to the right. It is possible to exit through this opening, though not without some risk.

Below us, six or seven meters down, a decorated chamber with some columns of considerable beauty can be seen. Once inside it, we can take some time to photograph it. In this chamber, we can appreciate that on the right side, straight ahead, and on the left, it is decorated with beautiful flowstones as well as some interesting columns. It is also possible to find a large quantity of bones in the area, undoubtedly due to animals that have accidentally fallen through the aforementioned opening and, being unable to leave the cave, have died inside it, or some of them simply died from the fall. I don't know what this chamber is called, if it has a name, but if it does, I wouldn't be surprised if it were the "Chamber of Bones."

To continue the visit, we must descend a steep ramp — not dangerous — seeking the deepest part until reaching a sort of arch that allows us to continue descending until we arrive at a chamber of approximately 20 meters in length, where there is a narrow lake of about 15 meters in length with crystal-clear waters along the left edge of the cave wall, which can be descended to without great difficulty though with some caution, as the terrain is slippery.

Once we return from the lake, the cave continues straight ahead in the direction we were going, beginning a gentle slope to finally have to use our hands to climb until we reach firm ground and continue advancing through narrow crawlways until reaching a sort of labyrinth where ropes (guides) appear that have been abandoned, possibly by people who previously explored the cave for its cartography.

In 2014, new galleries were found, to which the first and second photos of this work belong. We have also incorporated the topography surveyed by J. A. Encinas. To access this area, the corresponding permission must be requested, as a gate with its corresponding padlock protects it from unauthorized visits.
